b:Td45,<div class="specification">
<p><strong>Source A</strong> Paul Lococo Jr, an historian specializing in Chinese military history, writing in the academic book <em>Genghis Khan: History’s Greatest Empire Builder</em> (2008).</p>
<p>As we have seen already, alliances on the steppes were almost always fragile, as leaders wanted to gain benefits for themselves and their clans, as well as protection from other Mongol alliances. Over the years, Chinggis [Genghis Khan] demonstrated military and political skill, and he also acquired a reputation as a fair judge. His image was greatly enhanced through the use of shamans [spiritual leaders] who spread tales of omens favourable to Chinggis. The tribes often conducted successful raids, and Chinggis changed the way in which the rewards of the raids were divided. Traditionally, the various tribe and clan leaders divided the goods they had taken. Chinggis required that all plunder be placed in a common pile, and he then distributed it between the leaders, who in turn rewarded their followers. In this way, Chinggis not only created a better means of dividing the rewards of war, but he also emphasized his own supreme leadership. All rewards were granted by Chinggis, thus reinforcing his role as leader and his direct connection with the common Mongol soldier.</p>
<p style="text-align: center;">[Source: Adapted from <em>Genghis Khan: History’s Greatest Empire Builder</em> by Paul Lococo, Jr, by permission of the<br/>University of Nebraska Press. Copyright 2008 by Paul Lococo, Jr. Published by Potomac Books, Inc.]</p>
</div><div class="specification">
<p><strong>Source B</strong> George Lane, a professor of the history of the Middle East and Central Asia, writing in the academic book <em>Genghis Khan and Mongol Rule</em> (2004).</p>
<p>As head of the tribe, the chief was concerned mainly with the allocation of pastures, the planning of migration times and routes, and decisive leadership. The shaman and the other nobles, effectively a tribal council, both confirmed and provided limits to the chief’s authority. The tribe operated on two administrative levels. At the higher level was the tribal chief, who had direct control over the tribal nobles. At the lower level, these individual nobles controlled their own followers. It was sometimes in the tribe’s interest to form alliances or to seek the protection of a stronger tribe …</p>
<p>As a chief, Chinggis Khan built his power base and his tribal empire through battle, and with each victory he added more men to his army and more people to his following. However, those independent-minded tribal chiefs remained with him not from fear but from choice. Chinggis Khan offered his followers rewards and plenty of plunder. The tribes responded to his call and surrendered their independence because they believed that they would gain and prosper in his service. If he had not delivered, his support would have soon dissolved. Both his authority and the tribes’ continued support depended on his victories and political and military success. The charisma of the leader was crucial.</p>
<p style="text-align: center;">[Source: Lane, G., 2004. <em>Genghis Khan and Mongol Rule</em>. Indianapolis and Cambridge: Hackett, pp. 5–6. Adapted.]</p>
</div><div class="question">
<p>Compare and contrast what Sources A and B reveal about the leadership of Genghis Khan.</p>
</div>c:Tb381,<div class="question">
<p><strong><img src="data:image/png;base64,"/></strong></p>
<p><em>Apply the markbands that provide the “<strong>best fit</strong>” to the responses given by candidates and award credit <strong><span style="text-decoration:underline;">wherever</span> it is possible to do so</strong>. The following material is an indication of what candidates <strong>may</strong> elect to write about in their responses. It is neither prescriptive nor exhaustive and <strong>no set answer is required</strong></em>.</p>
<p><strong>Comparison</strong>:</p>
<ul>
<li>Both sources highlight Genghis Khan’s military and political skills as important factors as a powerful leader.</li>
<li>Both sources state that he offered plunder to his followers.</li>
<li>Both sources claim that Genghis Khan’s rule was dependent on continued success.</li>
<li>Both sources highlight the role of shamans in supporting Genghis Khan’s leadership.</li>
</ul>
<p><strong>Contrast</strong>:</p>
<ul>
<li>Source A highlights Genghis Khan’s supreme leadership whereas Source B states that nobles had direct control over their followers.</li>
<li>Source A claims shamans were used to promote Genghis Khan’s leadership favourably whereas Source B states that shamans provided some limits to the chief’s authority.</li>
</ul>
</div>2:["$","article",null,{"className":"q-page","children":[["$","p",null,{"style":{"margin":0},"children":["$","$L6",null,{"href":"/subject/history/topic/1/","children":["← back to ","History"," topic ","1"]}]}],["$","h1",null,{"children":"21N.1.BP.TZ0.3"}],["$","div",null,{"className":"q-meta","children":[["$","span",null,{"className":"tag","children":"pestle"}],["$","span",null,{"children":"History"}],null,null,null,null,["$","span",null,{"children":["· ","case-study-1-genghis-khan-c1200-1227"]}],["$","a",null,{"href":"https://pestle.pages.dev/app/","target":"_blank","rel":"noopener","children":"source ↗"}]]}],["$","div",null,{"className":"q-content","dangerouslySetInnerHTML":{"__html":"$b"}}],["$","details",null,{"className":"solution","children":[["$","summary",null,{"children":"Markscheme / solution"}],["$","div",null,{"dangerouslySetInnerHTML":{"__html":"$c"}}]]}],["$","details",null,{"className":"solution","children":[["$","summary",null,{"children":"Examiners’ report"}],["$","div",null,{"dangerouslySetInnerHTML":{"__html":"<div class=\"question\">\n<p>If was again pleasing to note that responses have continued to improve for the third question this session. Most candidates clearly identified comparisons and/or contrasts and had attempted to write a running commentary of similarities and differences. Indeed, there were some excellent responses to this question, usually characterised by precise references to the sources in support of the comparisons and/or contrasts that the candidate had identified. Nevertheless, some responses lacked clarity or offered only one or two links between the sources. As with the second question, some responses were presented in note-form and lacked development. Candidates should be made aware that, in order to access the top markband, more than two developed links between the sources should be established, for example two comparisons and two contrasts with development.</p>\n</div>"}}]]}]]}]
